message.content:
## Adversarial Claim Verifier (voter 2/3)

Be SKEPTICAL. Try to REFUTE this claim. ≥2/3 refutations kill it.

## Research question
BEST LOCAL / OPEN-SOURCE TEXT-TO-SPEECH (TTS) MODELS TO REPLACE OR AUGMENT KOKORO for an English educational-podcast pipeline, as of 2026. Baseline to beat: Kokoro-82M (hexgrad/Kokoro-82M), currently run locally on Apple Silicon (CPU + MPS), producing 24kHz audio, multiple voices (af_heart/am_onyx/bf_isabella/bm_george etc.), American + British English, two-voice format (narrator + question voice). Evaluate candidates across these angles: (1) QUALITY/NATURALNESS — which open/local TTS models beat Kokoro on naturalness and intelligibility for English (candidates to assess: XTTS-v2/Coqui, Piper, StyleTTS2, F5-TTS, Fish-Speech/OpenAudio, Parler-TTS, MeloTTS, Orpheus-TTS, Chatterbox, Kitten-TTS, Zonos, and any newer 2025-2026 open models) — report MOS/quality benchmarks, license (commercial/redistribution OK?), and model size. (2) WORD/PHONEME-LEVEL TIMING & ALIGNMENT — which models natively output word/phoneme timestamps (durations), and what forced-alignment tools (Montreal Forced Aligner, WhisperX, aeneas, ctc-forced-aligner) best produce a text↔audio timing map from existing audio — this enables content-aware speed compression, script-highlighting, and a true text-to-audio map. (3) HIGH-SPEED COMPREHENSIBILITY — which model's output stays most intelligible under aggressive time-compression / at high playback speed (crisp consonants, clean formant transitions); also the best FORMANT/robotic option (eSpeak-NG, RHVoice, DECtalk-like) for an extreme 16x 'ultra' track. (4) MULTI-VOICE & VOICE CONTROL — voice variety, voice cloning, prosody/rate control, and ease of adding new voices for a narrator+question two-speaker format. (5) LOCAL-INFERENCE PRACTICALITY on Apple Silicon and CPU — real-time factor / batch render speed, RAM/VRAM needs, MPS/CoreML/ONNX support, latency, and integration effort to swap into a Python pipeline that today calls Kokoro. For EACH recommended model give: quality vs Kokoro, license, local-inference feasibility on a Mac, whether it emits alignment, and a clear verdict on whether it's worth switching to (or adding alongside Kokoro). Prefer authoritative/benchmark sources and recent (2025-2026) comparisons; flag where claims are vendor marketing vs independent evaluation.

## Claim under review
"At identical overall compression rates, listener comprehension of nonuniformly (Mach1) compressed speech is 5 to 31 percentage points higher than linearly/uniformly compressed speech, with 15% faster response times."

**Source:** https://www.mangolassi.org/covell/1997-061/index.html (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "At identical overall compression rates, listener comprehension for Mach1-compressed speech increased between 5 and 31 percentage points over that for linearly compressed speech, and response times dropped by 15%."

## Checklist
1. Is the claim actually supported by the quote, or is it an overreach/misread?
2. WebSearch for contradicting evidence — does any credible source dispute or heavily qualify this?
3. Is the source quality sufficient for the claim's strength? (extraordinary claims need primary sources)
4. Is the claim outdated? (check dates — old claims about fast-moving fields are suspect)
5. Is this a marketing claim / press release / cherry-picked benchmark / forum speculation?

**refuted=true** if: unsupported by quote / contradicted / low-quality source for strong claim / outdated / marketing fluff.
**refuted=false** ONLY if: claim is well-supported, current, and source quality matches claim strength.
Default to refuted=true if uncertain.

Structured output only. Evidence MUST be specific.

- agents-sdk: Build AI agents on Cloudflare Workers using the Agents SDK. Load when creating stateful agents, durable workflows, real-time WebSocket apps, scheduled tasks, MCP servers, chat applications, voice agents, or browser automation. Covers Agent class, state management, callable RPC, Workflows, durable execution, queues, retries, observability, and React hooks. Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage ~75% by speaking like caveman while keeping full technical accuracy. Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.
- cloudflare: Comprehensive Cloudflare platform skill covering Workers, Pages, storage (KV, D1, R2), AI (Workers AI, Vectorize, Agents SDK), feature flags (Flagship), networking (Tunnel, Spectrum), security (WAF, DDoS), and infrastructure-as-code (Terraform, Pulumi). Use for any Cloudflare development task. Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- cloudflare-email-service: Send and receive transactional emails with Cloudflare Email Service (Email Sending + Email Routing). Use when building email sending (Workers binding or REST API), email routing, Agents SDK email handling, or integrating email into any app — Workers, Node.js, Python, Go, etc. Also use for email deliverability, SPF/DKIM/DMARC, wrangler email setup, MCP email tools, or when a coding agent needs to send emails. Even for simple requests like "add email to my Worker" — this skill has critical config details.

Use when designing, configuring, troubleshooting, or reviewing Cloudflare One deployments. Retrieval-first: use current Cloudflare docs/API schemas instead of embedded product docs.

Covers Sandbox SDK lifecycle, commands, files, code interpreter, and preview URLs. Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.

Load this when a user asks to add Turnstile, set up CAPTCHA, protect a form from bots, or fix a Turnstile integration. Mirrors developers.cloudflare.com/turnstile/spin.

Measures Core Web Vitals (LCP, INP, CLS) and supplementary metrics (FCP, TBT, Speed Index), identifies render-blocking resources, network dependency chains, layout shifts, caching issues, and accessibility gaps. Use when asked to audit, profile, debug, or optimize page load performance, Lighthouse scores, or site speed. Biases towards retrieval from current documentation over pre-trained knowledge.

Load before running wrangler commands to ensure correct syntax and best practices. Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

content.text: I'll verify this claim about Mach1 non-uniform speech compression. Let me start by examining the primary source.

input.prompt: Find any statements about Mach1 speech compression comprehension results. Specifically: does it say listener comprehension for Mach1-compressed speech increased between 5 and 31 percentage points over linearly compressed speech, and that response times dropped by 15%? Quote the exact relevant sentences.

input.query: Mach1 speech compression Covell comprehension percentage points response time

content.content:
# Mach1 Comprehension Results

Yes, the document confirms both claims. Here are the exact relevant sentences:

**On comprehension improvement:**
"At identical overall compression rates, listener comprehension for Mach1-compressed speech increased between 5 and 31 percentage points over that for linearly compressed speech, and response times dropped by 15%."

This statement appears in the Abstract section and directly matches your question.

Web search results for query: "Mach1 speech compression Covell comprehension percentage points response time"

Links: [{"title":"Michele Covell","url":"https://www.mangolassi.org/covell/index.html"},{"title":"Michele Covell - Mango Lassi","url":"https://www.mangolassi.org/covell/"},{"title":"Mach1 for Nonuniform Time-Scale Modification of Speech: Theory, Technique, and Comparisons","url":"https://engineering.purdue.edu/~malcolm/interval/1997-061/"},{"title":"Mach1 for Nonuniform Time-Scale Modification of Speech: Theory, Technique, and Comparisons","url":"https://www.mangolassi.org/covell/1997-061/index.html"},{"title":"Exploring benefits of non-linear time compression | Request PDF","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/221571389_Exploring_benefits_of_non-linear_time_compression"},{"title":"Intelligibility of time-compressed synthetic speech: Compression method and speaking style | Request PDF","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/282424493_Intelligibility_of_time-compressed_synthetic_speech_Compression_method_and_speaking_style"},{"title":"Mach1: Nonuniform Time-Scale Modification of Speech","url":"https://www.mangolassi.org/covell/1997-060/"},{"title":"(PDF) Novel Techniques For Time-Compressing Speech: An Exploratory Study","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/4136835_Novel_Techniques_For_Time-Compressing_Speech_An_Exploratory_Study"},{"title":"MACH1: nonuniform time-scale modification of speech | IEEE Conference Publication | IEEE Xplore","url":"https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/document/674439/"},{"title":"INTELLIGIBILITY OF TIME-COMPRESSED SPEECH: THREE WAYS OF TIME-COMPRESSION","url":"https://www.isca-archive.org/icslp_2000/janse00b_icslp.pdf"}]

Based on the search results, here are the key findings regarding Mach1 speech compression research by Covell and colleagues:

## Comprehension Improvements

At identical overall compression rates, listener comprehension for Mach1-compressed speech increased between 5 and 31 percentage points over that for linearly compressed speech, and response times dropped by 15%.

## Performance at High Compression Rates

For rates between 2.5 and 4.2 times real time, there was no significant comprehension loss with increasing Mach1 compression rates. Additionally, at compression rates between 2.5 and 4.2 times real time, comprehension of Mach1-compressed speech is 17 percentage points better than that of linearly compressed speech.

## Listener Preference

In A-B preference tests, Mach1-compressed speech was chosen 95% of the time.

## Design Approach

Mach1 is a new approach to nonuniform time compression for speech designed to mimic the natural timing of fast speech. Mach1 compresses the components of an utterance to resemble closely the natural timing of fast speech.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

content.text: The primary source confirms the claim verbatim. The supporting quote matches the source's abstract exactly. Now let me check whether independent sources corroborate or contradict this, since the source is the authors' own work (potential self-promotion). Let me look at independent evaluations.

Web search results for query: "non-linear time compression speech comprehension Mach1 independent replication study"

Links: [{"title":"Mach1 for Nonuniform Time-Scale Modification of Speech: Theory, Technique, and Comparisons","url":"https://engineering.purdue.edu/~malcolm/interval/1997-061/"},{"title":"MACH1: nonuniform time-scale modification of speech | IEEE Conference Publication | IEEE Xplore","url":"https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/document/674439/"},{"title":"(PDF) Novel Techniques For Time-Compressing Speech: An Exploratory Study","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/4136835_Novel_Techniques_For_Time-Compressing_Speech_An_Exploratory_Study"},{"title":"Exploring benefits of non-linear time compression | Request PDF","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/221571389_Exploring_benefits_of_non-linear_time_compression"},{"title":"Intelligibility of time-compressed synthetic speech: Compression method and speaking style - ScienceDirect","url":"https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0167639315000977"},{"title":"Word perception in fast speech: artificially time-compressed vs. naturally produced fast speech - ScienceDirect","url":"https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0167639303001092"},{"title":"Mach1: Nonuniform Time-Scale Modification of Speech","url":"https://www.mangolassi.org/covell/1997-060/"},{"title":"The effects of time compression on the comprehension of natural and synthetic speech | Request PDF","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/228339419_The_effects_of_time_compression_on_the_comprehension_of_natural_and_synthetic_speech"},{"title":"Non linear time compression of clear and normal speech at high rates","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/1901.07239"}]

Based on the web search results, here's what I found regarding non-linear time compression, speech comprehension, and Mach1:

## Original Mach1 Research

Mach1 is a new approach to nonuniform time compression designed to mimic the natural timing of fast speech. At identical overall compression rates, listener comprehension for Mach1-compressed speech increased between 5 and 31 percentage points over that for linearly compressed speech, and the response times dropped by 15%. For rates between 2.5 and 4.2 times real time, there was no significant comprehension loss with increasing Mach1 compression rates.

## Independent Replication Studies

The search results reveal mixed findings from independent replications of Mach1's effectiveness:

In contrast to the advantage of the mimicked natural fast speech by Mach1 observed by Covell et al. (1998) and He and Gupta (2001), Adank and Janse (2009) found that listeners exhibited greater difficulties when processing natural-fast speech than time-compressed speech.

Additionally, at ultra fast speaking rates (2.5 and 4.1 times) MACH1 improves comprehension and is preferable to linearly compressed speech but no advantage was found at the fast speech speaking rate (1.4 times).

These results suggest that while Mach1's benefits were demonstrated in initial studies, independent replications have shown more nuanced and sometimes contradictory findings, particularly regarding conditions where Mach1 provides advantages over linear compression.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

content.text: The search reveals important nuance. The claim is supported by the primary source verbatim, and at least one independent study (He & Gupta 2001) corroborated it. However, there are qualifications: Adank & Janse (2009) found contradictory results in some conditions, and the benefit appears to be limited to ultra-fast rates (2.5-4.1x) with no advantage at moderate speeds (1.4x). Let me verify the original source/authorship and the He & Gupta corroboration.

input.query: Covell Withgott Slaney Mach1 1998 nonuniform time compression speech authors paper
He Gupta 2001 exploring benefits non-linear time compression speech comprehension findings

content.content:
Web search results for query: "Covell Withgott Slaney Mach1 1998 nonuniform time compression speech authors paper"

Links: [{"title":"Mach1 for Nonuniform Time-Scale Modification of Speech: Theory, Technique, and Comparisons","url":"https://www.mangolassi.org/covell/1997-061/index.html"},{"title":"Mach1 for Nonuniform Time-Scale Modification of Speech: Theory, Technique, and Comparisons","url":"https://engineering.purdue.edu/~malcolm/interval/1997-061/writeup.html"},{"title":"(PDF) Novel Techniques For Time-Compressing Speech: An Exploratory Study","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/4136835_Novel_Techniques_For_Time-Compressing_Speech_An_Exploratory_Study"},{"title":"[PDF] Word perception in fast speech: artificially time-compressed vs. naturally produced fast speech | Semantic Scholar","url":"https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/Word-perception-in-fast-speech:-artificially-vs.-Janse/e6d93d9bd9bbda8268e21a425250e660cdecfdf2"},{"title":"Exploring benefits of non-linear time compression | Request PDF","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/221571389_Exploring_benefits_of_non-linear_time_compression"},{"title":"Intelligibility of time-compressed speech: The effect of uniform versus non-uniform time-compression algorithmsa) | The Journal of the Acoustical Society of America | AIP Publishing","url":"https://pubs.aip.org/asa/jasa/article-abstract/135/3/1541/693975/Intelligibility-of-time-compressed-speech-The?redirectedFrom=fulltext"},{"title":"Mach1: Nonuniform Time-Scale Modification of Speech","url":"https://www.mangolassi.org/covell/1997-060/"},{"title":"(PDF) Application of the DYPSA algorithm to segmented time-scale modification of speech","url":"https://www.academia.edu/2742843/Application_of_the_DYPSA_algorithm_to_segmented_time_scale_modification_of_speech"},{"title":"GitHub - google/speedy: Speedy Non-linear Speech Speedup Algorithm · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/google/speedy"}]

Based on the search results, I found comprehensive information about the paper you're looking for:

## Paper Details

The paper "Mach1: Nonuniform Time-Scale Modification of Speech" was authored by Michele Covell, Margaret Withgott, and Malcolm Slaney from Interval Research Corporation. The work was published at the IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech, and Signal Processing in Seattle WA, May 12-15 1998.

## About the Research

The authors proposed a new approach to nonuniform time compression, called Mach1, designed to mimic the natural timing of fast speech. At identical overall compression rates, listener comprehension for Mach1-compressed speech increased between 5 and 31 percentage points over that for linearly compressed speech, and response times dropped by 15%. For rates between 2.5 and 4.2 times real time, there was no significant comprehension loss with increasing Mach1 compression rates.

The technical report accompanying this conference paper was identified as IRC-TR 1997-061, with additional details available in the original technical documentation.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Web search results for query: "He Gupta 2001 exploring benefits non-linear time compression speech comprehension findings"

Links: [{"title":"Exploring benefits of non-linear time compression | Request PDF","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/221571389_Exploring_benefits_of_non-linear_time_compression"},{"title":"Word perception in fast speech: artificially time-compressed vs. naturally produced fast speech - ScienceDirect","url":"https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0167639303001092"},{"title":"Intelligibility of time-compressed synthetic speech: Compression method and speaking style - ScienceDirect","url":"https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0167639315000977"},{"title":"The effects of time-compressed instruction and redundancy on learning and learners' perceptions of cognitive load | Computers & Education","url":"https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1016/j.compedu.2011.09.018"},{"title":"Interactive speech skimming via time-stretched audio replay | Request PDF","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/287057652_Interactive_speech_skimming_via_time-stretched_audio_replay"},{"title":"Exploring benefits of non-linear time compression | Proceedings of the ninth ACM international conference on Multimedia","url":"https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/500141.500198"},{"title":"(PDF) Novel Techniques For Time-Compressing Speech: An Exploratory Study","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/4136835_Novel_Techniques_For_Time-Compressing_Speech_An_Exploratory_Study"},{"title":"On-line plasticity in spoken sentence comprehension: Adapting to time-compressed speech","url":"https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2775905/"}]

I found search results for the He and Gupta 2001 study on non-linear time compression and speech comprehension. Here are the key findings:

## Study Overview

He and Gupta (2001) published "Exploring benefits of non-linear time compression" in the Proceedings of the ACM international conference on multimedia in Ottawa, Canada (pp. 382-391).

## Key Findings

**Compression Methods and Rates:**
They investigated a synchronous overlap-add method and a modified version of Mach1 and compressed speech down to 40% of their original length (unprocessed speech: 249-286 syll/min, processed speech: up to 623-714 syll/min).

**Comprehension Results:**
They tested various linear and nonlinear compression techniques and found that, regardless of the sophistication of the algorithm, the participants reached a ceiling level. At ultra fast speaking rates (2.5 and 4.1 times) MACH1 improves comprehension and is preferable to linearly compressed speech but no advantage was found at the fast speech speaking rate (1.4 times).

**Listening Sustainability:**
The study suggests that the future bottleneck for consuming multimedia contents will not be network bandwidth but people's limited time, and they found that an upper bound for sustainable speedup during continuous listening is at about 1.6 to 1.7 times the normal speed.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

content.text:
I now have enough to make a determination. Let me assess against the checklist.

**Findings:**

1. **Claim supported by quote?** Yes, exactly. The claim is a verbatim restatement of the abstract of the primary source: Covell, Withgott & Slaney (1998), "Mach1: Nonuniform Time-Scale Modification of Speech," IEEE ICASSP, with technical report IRC-TR 1997-061. The supporting quote matches the source word-for-word.

2. **Source quality?** This is the primary, original peer-reviewed source — an IEEE ICASSP 1998 conference paper from Interval Research Corporation, not a forum post or marketing. Appropriate for the claim.

3. **Contradicting/qualifying evidence?** The figures themselves are not disputed, but the claim as stated is the authors' own result reporting their best-case range. Independent work qualifies the generalization:
   - He & Gupta (2001) corroborated that Mach1 improves comprehension over linear compression — but only at ultra-fast rates (2.5x and 4.1x); **no advantage at 1.4x** (moderate speed).
   - Adank & Janse (2009) found listeners had more difficulty with natural-fast speech than time-compressed speech, contrasting with the premise.

4. **Outdated?** The claim is about an empirical psychoacoustic result (1998), not a fast-moving field. The underlying finding remains the canonical citation and has been partially replicated. Not outdated in a refuting sense.

5. **Marketing/cherry-picked?** The "5 to 31 percentage points" is a wide range representing the authors' own results. It's an accurate quote of the paper but is the upper-and-lower envelope of their conditions; the "15% faster response times" is also the authors' figure. It's legitimate academic reporting, not vendor marketing.

**Verdict:** The claim is an accurate, faithful quotation of a legitimate primary peer-reviewed source. The numbers are correctly attributed and not misrepresented. The main caveat is that the claim presents a best-case range as if universal, when independent replication shows the benefit is rate-dependent (strong at ultra-fast 2.5-4x, negligible at ~1.4x). But that nuance qualifies the *generalization*, not the literal claim — which is exactly what the paper reported. The claim is well-supported, source quality matches strength, and it is corroborated (not contradicted) on its core assertion. This does not meet the bar for refutation.

input.evidence: The claim is a verbatim quote of the abstract of the original peer-reviewed primary source: Covell, Withgott & Slaney (1998), "Mach1: Nonuniform Time-Scale Modification of Speech," IEEE ICASSP, Seattle (tech report IRC-TR 1997-061), from Interval Research Corporation. WebFetch of https://www.mangolassi.org/covell/1997-061/index.html returned the exact sentence: "At identical overall compression rates, listener comprehension for Mach1-compressed speech increased between 5 and 31 percentage points over that for linearly compressed speech, and response times dropped by 15%." The claim matches word-for-word, so it is supported by the quote (not an overreach). Source quality is appropriate: an IEEE conference paper, not a forum post or marketing. The core finding is CORROBORATED, not contradicted, by an independent study (He & Gupta 2001, "Exploring benefits of non-linear time compression," ACM Multimedia), which found Mach1 improves comprehension and is preferable to linear compression at ultra-fast rates (2.5x and 4.1x). Caveat that qualifies the GENERALIZATION (not the literal claim): He & Gupta found NO advantage at moderate 1.4x speed, and Adank & Janse (2009) found listeners struggled more with natural-fast speech than time-compressed speech in some conditions. So the benefit is rate-dependent rather than universal. But the literal claim — that the paper reports a 5-31pp comprehension gain and 15% faster response times — is accurately attributed to a credible primary source and is partially replicated. The "5 to 31 percentage points" is a wide best-/worst-case envelope from the authors' own conditions, which is academic result reporting, not vendor marketing. Does not meet the refutation bar.
input.counterSource: He & Gupta (2001), "Exploring benefits of non-linear time compression," ACM Multimedia (corroborates at 2.5x/4.1x but finds no advantage at 1.4x); Adank & Janse (2009), ScienceDirect S0167639303001092 (natural-fast harder than time-compressed). These qualify the generalization but do not refute the literal quoted figures.

content.text:
I've completed the verification. The claim is **not refuted** (refuted=false) with high confidence.

The claim is a verbatim, accurate quote of the abstract of the original peer-reviewed primary source — Covell, Withgott & Slaney (1998), "Mach1: Nonuniform Time-Scale Modification of Speech," IEEE ICASSP (tech report IRC-TR 1997-061). The numbers ("5 to 31 percentage points" and "15% faster response times") are correctly attributed and not misrepresented. The source quality (IEEE conference paper) matches the claim's strength, and the core finding is corroborated by an independent study (He & Gupta 2001), not contradicted. The only caveat is that the benefit is rate-dependent (strong at ultra-fast 2.5-4x, negligible at ~1.4x per independent replication) — but this qualifies the generalization, not the literal claim, which faithfully reports what the paper found.
